planting.note: Direct sow fresh seeds in fall in sandy, well-drained, acidic to neutral soil in full sun; seeds naturally cold-stratify over winter and germinate in spring. Established corms can be divided in fall and replanted immediately. Gulf Blazingstar is highly specific to dry, sandy soils and will not thrive in clay or poorly drained locations.

directions: Gulf Blazingstar is one of the most distinctive and beautiful of the southeastern blazingstars, native to the dry, sandy soils of longleaf pine savannas, coastal plain sandhills, and open flatwoods from North Carolina to Texas along the Gulf and South Atlantic coastal plain. The feathery, pinkish-purple flower heads are fringed by conspicuously elongated, spreading scales (phyllaries) that give each cluster a soft, paintbrush-like appearance that immediately sets it apart from the tighter, more cylindrical heads of most other Liatris species. Blooming from September through November, Gulf Blazingstar is a critical late-season nectar source for migrating monarch butterflies, native bumblebees, and sphinx moths preparing for winter. Direct sow fresh seeds in fall in sandy, infertile, well-drained, acidic soil in full sun; the corms that form are persistent and long-lived, eventually forming impressive clumps that can be divided every 3–4 years.
sources: Florida Wildflowers in Their Natural Communities (Walter Kingsley Taylor)

planting.note: Direct sow fresh seeds in fall in pure sandy, well-drained, infertile, acidic soil in full sun; Florida Blazingstar is a Florida endemic best suited to xeric scrub and flatwoods conditions. The small corms are slow to establish but persistent once settled in appropriate soil.

directions: Florida Blazingstar is a small native perennial endemic to the sandy scrub, sandhill, and dry flatwood communities of the Florida peninsula, producing slender spikes of small, pinkish-purple flower heads in fall that provide a vital late-season nectar source for native bees, skippers, and migrating monarchs in one of the most imperiled native plant communities in the United States. Adapted to the infertile, extremely well-drained, white-sand soils of Florida's ancient scrub ridges, this compact blazingstar forms tight clumps from persistent corms in habitats that most other plants cannot tolerate. Like other Florida scrub plants, it depends on periodic, low-intensity fire to maintain the open, sunny conditions it requires. Direct sow fresh seeds in fall in pure, infertile, sandy, acidic soil in full sun; established plants require no supplemental irrigation or fertilization in appropriate xeric conditions.
sources: Florida Wildflowers in Their Natural Communities (Walter Kingsley Taylor)

planting.note: Direct sow fresh seeds in fall in sandy, well-drained, acidic, infertile soil in full sun; seeds cold-stratify naturally over winter and germinate in spring. Southern Blazingstar thrives in the longleaf pine savanna and coastal flatwood conditions of the southeastern coastal plain.

directions: Southern Blazingstar is a small, slender native perennial of the sandy, open longleaf pine savannas, flatwoods, and coastal plain prairies of the Gulf and South Atlantic states from Georgia to Louisiana, producing dense wand-like spikes of small pink-purple flower heads in early fall that attract monarch butterflies, skippers, and native bees during the critical fall migration period. Less showy than the larger Liatris species but ecologically significant in its native longleaf pine ecosystem, Southern Blazingstar is a component of the diverse herbaceous layer that characterizes intact coastal plain savannas. Direct sow fresh seeds in fall in sandy, infertile, acidic, well-drained soil in full sun; established corms are drought-tolerant and long-lived, forming tight clumps that slowly expand over time in appropriate open, sunny conditions.
sources: Florida Wildflowers in Their Natural Communities (Walter Kingsley Taylor)

planting.note: Direct sow fresh seeds in fall in dry, rocky or sandy, well-drained, acidic to neutral soil in full sun; seeds cold-stratify naturally over winter and germinate in spring. Scaly Blazingstar tolerates rocky, calcareous, and sandy soils and is more drought-tolerant than most blazingstar species.

directions: Scaly Blazingstar is a distinctive native perennial of dry, rocky or sandy, open woodlands, prairies, glades, and barrens across the eastern United States from New England south to Florida and west to Nebraska, immediately recognizable among blazingstars by the squarrose (stiffly spreading) tips of its phyllary bracts — the scales surrounding each flower head — that curve sharply outward, giving the flower head a rough, star-like appearance. The large, intensely purple flower heads are produced from July through September on stiff, upright stems, providing outstanding nectar for migrating monarch butterflies, native bumblebees, sphinx moths, and many species of native bees and butterflies. Adapted to lean, infertile soils in full sun, Scaly Blazingstar is longer-lived and more reliably perennial than some blazingstars when grown in appropriate dry, xeric conditions. Direct sow fresh seeds in fall in dry, rocky or sandy, well-drained, acidic to neutral soil; established corms multiply slowly to form impressive, long-lived clumps.

planting.note: Direct sow fresh seeds in fall in moist to moderately dry, sandy to loamy, acidic, well-drained soil in full sun; Sharp Blazingstar tolerates somewhat moister soils than many blazingstars and thrives in the coastal prairie and savanna conditions of Texas and Louisiana where periodic seasonal flooding alternates with drought.

directions: Sharp Blazingstar is a native perennial of the Gulf Coast prairies, savannas, and open meadows of Texas and Louisiana, producing dense spikes of pinkish-purple flower heads from late summer through fall that provide important nectar for migrating monarchs and native bees during the critical fall migration period along the Gulf Coast. Unlike many blazingstars that prefer dry, xeric conditions, Sharp Blazingstar tolerates moist prairie soils and the seasonal flooding that characterizes the Gulf Coast prairie ecosystem, making it suitable for a broader range of garden conditions than its dry-country relatives. The tight, upright spike of densely packed flower heads is similar in appearance to Prairie Blazingstar but more compact in stature. Direct sow fresh seeds in fall in moist to moderately dry, sandy to loamy, acidic soil in full sun; corms develop slowly but become very drought-tolerant once established in appropriate open conditions.
sources: Texas Native Plants Database (Ladybird Johnson Wildflower Center)

planting.note: Direct sow fresh seeds in fall in sandy, well-drained, infertile, acidic soil in full sun. Florida Goldenrod is a Florida endemic best suited to xeric scrub, sandhill, and flatwoods habitats; it does not thrive in clay, amended, or waterlogged soils. Established plants spread slowly by short rhizomes.

directions: Florida Goldenrod is a native perennial endemic to the sandy, xeric scrub, sandhill, and flatwoods habitats of the Florida peninsula, producing bright yellow flower spikes in fall that provide critical late-season nectar for native bees, monarch butterflies, and numerous other pollinators in the imperiled Florida scrub ecosystem. Distinctly compact compared to most goldenrods, with a more open, arching inflorescence suited to the sparse, sunny, sandy conditions of Florida's ancient scrub communities where many competing plants cannot grow. Like the other specialized plants of Florida's scrub, Florida Goldenrod depends on periodic, low-intensity fire to maintain the open, well-lit conditions it requires. Direct sow fresh seeds in fall in sandy, infertile, acidic, extremely well-drained soil in full sun; established plants are virtually maintenance-free and very drought-tolerant once the root system is established.

planting.note: Direct sow fresh seeds in fall in moist to moderately wet, sandy, acidic, poorly drained or seasonally wet soil in full sun to partial shade; Pine Barren Goldenrod is one of the few goldenrods that tolerates and prefers seasonally wet flatwoods and pine barren conditions. Established plants spread steadily by rhizomes.

directions: Pine Barren Goldenrod is a native perennial of the moist to seasonally wet sandy pine barrens, flatwoods, and boggy savannas of the Atlantic and Gulf coastal plain from New Jersey south to Florida and west to Louisiana, distinguished among goldenrods by its hollow, pithy stem (fistulosa means 'hollow' or 'pipe-like') and its tolerance for the wet, acidic, nutrient-poor soils of flatwood depressions and moist pine barrens. The bright yellow flower spikes in fall are an outstanding nectar source for monarch butterflies, native bees, and numerous other pollinators migrating or overwintering along the southeastern coastal plain. Spreading reliably by rhizomes to form colonies in appropriate conditions, Pine Barren Goldenrod is an excellent choice for wet meadow, rain garden, and moist flatwood restoration plantings. Direct sow fresh seeds in fall in moist to wet, sandy, acidic soil in full sun to partial shade; established plants naturalize readily in wet, open, seasonally flooded settings.
sources: Florida Wildflowers in Their Natural Communities (Walter Kingsley Taylor)

planting.note: Direct sow seeds on the surface of sandy, well-drained, infertile soil in full sun after all danger of frost has passed and soil has warmed to 70°F; do not cover seeds, as they require light to germinate. Pink Purslane self-seeds prolifically in sandy soil and may establish itself as a recurring volunteer in the garden.

directions: Pink Purslane is a native annual wildflower of the southeastern United States and tropical Americas, sprawling across sandy roadsides, coastal dunes, dry flatwoods openings, and disturbed sunny areas with tiny, vivid magenta-pink flowers that open in the morning sunshine and close by midday. A close relative of the Moss Rose (Portulaca grandiflora) sold in garden centers, Pink Purslane is smaller and more delicate but shares the same remarkable heat and drought tolerance that makes portulacas ideal for hot, dry, infertile spots where other flowering annuals fail. The fleshy, succulent leaves are edible — with a slightly sour, mucilaginous texture similar to common purslane — and were eaten by various indigenous peoples of the Americas. Direct sow seeds on the soil surface in full sun after soil warms to 70°F; Pink Purslane self-seeds prolifically in sandy, infertile, well-drained soil and may establish as a recurring garden volunteer.

planting.note: Plant offsets or direct sow seeds in spring in extremely well-drained, sandy to rocky, infertile, neutral to alkaline, coastal soil in full sun. False Sisal produces offsets (pups) freely around the base of the rosette — these are the most reliable propagation method. It is monocarpic, blooming once after 10–20 years then dying, but the offsets ensure colony persistence.

directions: False Sisal is a native agave of the coastal scrub, pine rocklands, and coastal dune communities of southern Florida and the Caribbean, producing a dramatic, architectural rosette of long, sword-like, blue-green leaves armed with sharp terminal spines and marginal teeth. Like all agaves, False Sisal is monocarpic — the main rosette blooms once after 10–20 years, sending up a spectacular branched flower stalk reaching 15–25 feet tall bearing thousands of pale yellow flowers that attract a spectacular diversity of moths, bats, and native bees, before the main rosette dies. However, the plant compensates by freely producing offsets (pups) around its base for years before flowering, ensuring colony persistence. Plant in extremely well-drained, sandy to rocky, infertile, neutral to slightly alkaline coastal soil in full sun; False Sisal is highly drought-tolerant and salt-tolerant once established but cannot tolerate frost, clay, or persistently wet conditions.

planting.note: Plant container-grown transplants in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, rocky or sandy, humus-rich soil in partial shade to full sun. Florida Clover Ash is a rare native restricted to Miami-Dade pine rocklands and is best suited to frost-free Zone 10–11 South Florida landscapes. It is available from specialty native plant nurseries.

directions: Florida Clover Ash — also called West Indian Clover Ash — is a native member of the Melastomataceae (melastome) family, found in the United States only in the pine rocklands and tropical hardwood hammocks of Miami-Dade County, Florida, where it is listed as critically imperiled. The lance-shaped, three-veined leaves are glossy and attractive; clusters of small white to pale pink flowers appear in spring and summer and are followed by dark purple-black berries eaten by birds including the White-crowned Pigeon. An ornamental and ecologically significant native shrub of the highly threatened South Florida pine rockland community — one of the most endangered ecosystems in the United States — Florida Clover Ash deserves wider cultivation in Zone 10–11 South Florida native plant gardens. Plant in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, rocky or sandy, humus-rich soil in partial shade to full sun; established plants are drought-tolerant in the rocky, thin soils of its native pine rockland habitat.
